name: Gallium
name_origin: Named after 'Gallia', the Latin name for France, by its discoverer Paul-Emile Lecoq de Boisbaudran in 1875; the name may also be a Latin pun on his own name ('le coq' = 'gallus' in Latin).

occurrence.abundance_summary: Gallium is moderately abundant in Earth's crust at roughly 19 ppm but rarely concentrated; it is recovered chiefly as a by-product of bauxite (aluminum) processing and zinc refining. Predicted by Mendeleev as 'eka-aluminum' before its discovery.
states: trace in zinc and germanium ores

compounds.name: Gallium arsenide
compounds.summary: A III-V semiconductor with a direct bandgap, used in high-frequency electronics, solar cells, and laser diodes.

compounds.name: Gallium nitride
compounds.summary: Wide-bandgap semiconductor essential to blue and white LEDs, Blu-ray lasers, and high-efficiency power transistors.

compounds.name: Gallium(III) oxide
compounds.summary: An ultra-wide-bandgap semiconductor under intense study for next-generation high-voltage power electronics.

compounds.name: Gallium(III) chloride
compounds.summary: Volatile Lewis acid used in organic synthesis and as a catalyst; precursor for many gallium compounds.

compounds.name: Gallium(III) hydroxide
compounds.summary: Amphoteric white solid that dissolves in both acids and bases; intermediate in gallium recovery from Bayer-process liquor.

uses: Semiconductors for LEDs, laser diodes, and RF amplifiers (GaAs, GaN)
Low-melting alloys (e.g., galinstan) used as non-toxic mercury replacements in thermometers
Medical imaging tracers (gallium-67 and gallium-68 for PET)
Wetting promoter for vacuum systems and specialty mirrors
reactivity_summary: Gallium is a soft silvery metal that melts just above room temperature (29.76 degrees Celsius) and can stay liquid up to about 2400 degrees Celsius, giving it the largest liquid range of any element. It tarnishes only slightly in air, forming a thin protective oxide layer, and dissolves in mineral acids and alkalis. Liquid gallium aggressively wets and embrittles many metals, including aluminum and steel.
isotopes_summary: Gallium has two stable isotopes: 69Ga (~60.1%) and 71Ga (~39.9%). About 30 radioisotopes are known; the most useful are 67Ga (half-life ~3.26 days), used in scintigraphy, and 68Ga (half-life ~68 minutes), used in PET imaging via generator systems.

citations.source: CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
